name: Ninurta
description: Ninurta, also known as Ningirsu, is a Sumerian god of agriculture, hunting, law, scribes, and war. He was primarily worshipped in Sumer. He is the son of Enlil and Ninlil, and his consort is Gula (also known as Bau). Ninurta is depicted as a powerful warrior, often wielding a mace or bow and arrow. He is a protector of boundaries and fields, and his victories against monstrous beings represent the triumph of order over chaos. He is also associated with the south wind and the plow. He defeated Asag, a monstrous demon, using his Sharur, an enchanted mace, securing fertile land for agriculture. Ninurta's role encompassed both benevolent guardianship and fierce combat, solidifying his importance in the Sumerian pantheon. He was revered for his strength, wisdom, and dedication to maintaining cosmic order. He is also seen as a divine farmer, ensuring bountiful harvests and prosperity for the land. His temple was located in the city of Girsu.
subtitle: Sumerian God of War and Agriculture

keyMyths.title: Ninurta and the Turtle
keyMyths.description: Enki sent a giant turtle to fight Ninurta, but Ninurta used his cunning and strength to defeat it. This highlighted Ninurta's strategic thinking and combat prowess.
keyMyths.source: Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature

keyMyths.title: Ninurta's Victory Over Asag
keyMyths.description: Ninurta defeated the monstrous Asag, who was poisoning the land, using his enchanted mace Sharur. This victory brought fertility and prosperity back to the land.
keyMyths.source: Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature

keyMyths.title: The Return to Nibru
keyMyths.description: After defeating Asag, Ninurta redirected the flow of the Tigris and Euphrates rivers, bringing water to the fields. He then returned to Nibru to receive praise from his father, Enlil.
keyMyths.source: Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature

sources.author: Jeremy Black and Anthony Green
sources.work: Gods, Demons and Symbols of Ancient Mesopotamia
sources.description: Comprehensive overview of Mesopotamian religion and mythology.
sources.text: Comprehensive overview of Mesopotamian religion and mythology.
